summary: German Romantic composer and music critic (1810–1856), married to pianist Clara Wieck. Known for his piano music (Carnaval, Kinderszenen, Kreisleriana, Fantasie in C, Davidsbündlertänze), song cycles (Dichterliebe, the two Liederkreis, A Woman's Love and Life), four symphonies (including the 'Spring' No. 1 and 'Rhenish' No. 3), and chamber works (Piano Quintet in E-flat, Piano Quartet). He expressed himself through the fictional alter egos Florestan (fiery) and Eusebius (lyrical). Founded the Neue Zeitschrift für Musik. Suffered a mental breakdown and died in an asylum in Endenich.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Robert Schumann (1810–1856) was a German Romantic composer, pianist, and critic. He married Clara Wieck in 1840 (the year of his famous 'Year of Song'). He ruined his right hand by using a finger-strengthening mechanism, ending his concert career. He co-founded the music journal Neue Zeitschrift für Musik, in which he wrote under the fictional personas of Florestan (fiery) and Eusebius (lyrical), along with Master Raro (representing his father-in-law Friedrich Wieck). He used these alter egos throughout his music, most famously in Carnaval and Davidsbündlertänze. He published 'New Paths' in 1853, championing the young Johannes Brahms. He suffered a mental breakdown, attempted suicide by jumping into the Rhine, and died in the asylum at Endenich. His Ghost Variations were his final composition; he thought the melody was dictated to him by ghosts (it was actually from his own D minor Violin Concerto). Wikipedia

clues.clue: Married Clara Wieck in 1840 (his 'Year of Song'); she premiered many of his works including the Piano Concerto and Piano Quintet

works.name: Carnaval (Op. 9)
works.description: Schumann's 1835 set of 21 piano miniatures (Op. 9) depicting a masked pre-Lent carnival. Uses permutations of the ASCH cryptogram (A-Eb-C-B or A-C-B, the letters of a town in Bohemia) throughout. Depicts commedia dell'arte characters (Pierrot, Arlequin, Pantaloon, Columbine) alongside real persons (Chiarina = Clara Wieck, Estrella = Ernestine von Fricken, Paganini, Chopin). Includes a section called 'Sphinxes' (three bars with no key signature or tempo — never meant to be played) and ends with a 'March of the Davidsbündler against the Philistines.' Florestan and Eusebius are both represented. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Set of 21 piano miniatures depicting a masked pre-Lent carnival; uses permutations of the ASCH cryptogram throughout

works.name: Dichterliebe (Op. 48)
works.description: Schumann's 1840 song cycle of 16 songs setting poems from Heinrich Heine's Lyrisches Intermezzo. Composed during his 'Year of Song.' The cycle opens with 'Im wunderschönen Monat Mai' (ending with an unresolved dominant 7th chord in F-sharp minor). Includes 'Ich grolle nicht' ('I bear no grudge'). The last song ('Die alten, bösen Lieder') has the poet imagining putting his songs and dreams in a huge coffin to be cast into the sea. Wikipedia

works.name: Kinderszenen (Scenes from Childhood, Op. 15)
works.description: Schumann's 1838 set of 13 short piano pieces depicting a child's world. Most famous movement is 'Träumerei' ('Dreaming/Reverie'), depicting a child's dream. Also contains 'Of Foreign Lands and Peoples,' 'Blind Man's Bluff,' and 'The Happy Farmer' (actually in Album for the Young, Op. 68). The suite is often associated with 'The Happy Farmer,' though that piece is technically from the Album for the Young. Wikipedia

clues.clue: 'Träumerei' ('Dreaming') — most famous movement, depicting a child's dream
examples: This composer of Carnaval depicted a young child dreaming in the 'Traumerei' movement of his Kinderszenen

works.name: Fantasie in C (Op. 17)
works.description: Schumann's 1838 three-movement piano fantasy. Originally titled 'Ruins'; the first movement opens with a C major theme and contains a mysterious 'Im Legenden-ton' middle section, followed by a coda quoting Beethoven's song cycle An die ferne Geliebte. Dedicated to Liszt. Wikipedia

clues.clue: First movement contains a 'Im Legenden-ton' section and ends with a coda quoting Beethoven's An die ferne Geliebte; originally titled 'Ruins'
examples: A coda that quotes An die ferne geliebte follows a mysterious Im Legenden-ton section in a musical love letter that this composer once titled Ruins, which opens his Fantasie in C
This composer included a minor-key episode marked 'Im Legendenton', or 'in the style of a legend', in the first movement of a three-movement piano fantasy in C major

works.name: Piano Concerto in A minor (Op. 54)
works.description: Schumann's only piano concerto, premiered by Clara Schumann in 1845. Began as a Concert Phantasie and was expanded into a full concerto. In A minor (same key as Clara's concerto and Grieg's concerto). Begins with a tutti chord, three bars of solo piano, two more tutti chords, and a woodwind chorale led by the oboe. First movement contains an andante espressivo section in A-flat major and 6/4 time. The main theme of the first movement contains a cipher for 'Chiara' (the Italian spelling of Clara). Often paired with Grieg's Piano Concerto. Wikipedia

clues.clue: In A minor; premiered by Clara Wieck; often paired with Grieg's A minor Piano Concerto
examples: This composer's piano concerto is often paired on recordings with a later piano concerto in the same key by Edvard Grieg

works.name: Piano Quintet in E-flat major (Op. 44)
works.description: Schumann's 1842 Piano Quintet in E-flat major, his most innovative chamber work. Premiered by Clara at the Leipzig Gewandhaus. First movement opens with the piano leaping up a minor seventh from E-flat to D-flat. Second movement is a funeral march in C minor with rapid descending scales. Last movement contains a double fugue integrating the opening theme of the first movement. Its use of two violins instead of a cello was innovative; Brahms's Piano Quintet was influenced by it. Wikipedia

works.name: Symphony No. 3 in E-flat major ("Rhenish")
works.description: Schumann's third symphony (1850), though numbered before the earlier D minor Symphony No. 4. Inspired by a trip with Clara to the Rhineland and witnessing the coronation of a cardinal at Cologne Cathedral. Five movements: the fourth movement features a solemn brass chorale in E-flat minor for trombones and horns (their first appearance in the symphony). Second movement is a Ländler (though does not actually quote folk songs). Wikipedia

clues.clue: Inspired by the Rhine/Rhineland; five movements; fourth movement has a solemn trombone chorale in E-flat minor (trombones' first entrance) inspired by Cologne Cathedral
examples: trombones enter for the first time at the start of the fourth movement, where they play a slow chorale in E-flat minor, the parallel key
A symphony titled for this location has a solemn trombone choir in its fourth movement, which was inspired by a ceremony Schumann witnessed at Cologne Cathedral

clues.clue: Five movements — unusual; second movement is a Ländler (no actual folk song quotes); numbered before the earlier D minor Symphony No. 4
examples: Although it was composed in 1850, this symphony is numbered before an earlier D-minor symphony
Although it does not actually quote any folk songs, the second of this symphony's five movements is titled Landler for a German folk dance

works.name: Other Works
works.description: Other major Schumann works: Symphony No. 1 ("Spring", B-flat major); Symphony No. 2 (C major — only homotonal 19th-century symphony, Mendelssohn conducted premiere, unusual scherzo with two trios); Symphony No. 4 (D minor, originally written before No. 2 and 3); Kreisleriana (8-movement piano suite after E.T.A. Hoffmann's character); Davidsbündlertänze (18 piano pieces); Album for the Young (Op. 68, includes 'The Happy Farmer'); Frauenliebe und -leben (A Woman's Love and Life, Chamisso poems); the two Liederkreis cycles (Op. 24 Heine, Op. 39 Eichendorff); Piano Quartet in E-flat; Manfred Overture.

comprehensive_summary:
Robert Schumann (1810–1856) was a German Romantic composer, pianist, and music critic. He married the pianist Clara Wieck in 1840, the same year he composed over 130 songs during his famous 'Year of Song' (Liederjahr). His performing career was ended when a finger-strengthening mechanism permanently injured his right hand. He co-founded the Neue Zeitschrift für Musik, writing criticism under the fictional personas of Florestan (fiery) and Eusebius (lyrical), with Master Raro representing his father-in-law Friedrich Wieck. He famously championed Chopin (Eusebius exclaims 'Hats off, gentlemen, a genius!' in a review of Chopin's variations on 'Là ci darem la mano') and later Brahms (in the 1853 essay 'New Paths'). He suffered from mental illness, attempted suicide by jumping into the Rhine, and died in the asylum at Endenich in 1856.

His piano music includes: Carnaval (Op. 9, 1835) — 21 miniatures on an ASCH cryptogram, depicting commedia dell'arte characters alongside 'Chiarina' (Clara), 'Estrella' (Ernestine von Fricken), Paganini, and Chopin; a 'Sphinxes' section (three bars with no markings, never played); ending with a 'March of the Davidsbündler'; Kinderszenen (Op. 15, 1838) — 13 children's pieces, most famous for 'Träumerei'; Fantasie in C (Op. 17, 1838) — three movements, 'Im Legenden-ton' section, coda quoting Beethoven's An die ferne Geliebte, originally titled 'Ruins'; Kreisleriana (Op. 16) — 8 pieces after E.T.A. Hoffmann's Kreisler character; the Toccata in C (called 'the hardest piece ever written'); and Ghost Variations (his last composition, melody unknowingly transposed from his own D minor Violin Concerto).

His Dichterliebe (Op. 48, 1840) sets 16 Heine poems: the opening 'Im wunderschönen Monat Mai' ends on an unresolved dominant 7th; 'Ich grolle nicht' has the singer expressing 'I bear no grudge' over nonstop C major chords; the final song imagines a giant coffin thrown into the sea. His two Liederkreis cycles set Heine (Op. 24) and Eichendorff (Op. 39) poems. A Woman's Love and Life (Op. 42) sets Chamisso poems.

His Piano Concerto in A minor (Op. 54) was premiered by Clara; it began as a Concert Phantasie, begins with a distinctive tutti-chord/solo piano/tutti-chord/woodwind-chorale opening, and contains a cipher for 'Chiara' in the main theme. It is routinely paired with Grieg's A minor concerto. His Piano Quintet in E-flat (Op. 44, 1842) — premiered by Clara at the Leipzig Gewandhaus — opens with a minor-seventh leap (E-flat to D-flat), has a funeral march second movement, and a finale with a double fugue; it influenced Brahms's Piano Quintet.

Among his four symphonies: the 'Spring' Symphony No. 1 (B-flat, modeled on Beethoven's 4th); Symphony No. 2 (C major — the only homotonal 19th-century symphony, Mendelssohn conducted the premiere, unusual scherzo with two trios); the 'Rhenish' Symphony No. 3 (E-flat, five movements, inspired by Cologne Cathedral, trombone chorale in E-flat minor in the fourth movement); and Symphony No. 4 (D minor, actually written earlier but numbered later). He also wrote the Album for the Young (Op. 68, 'The Happy Farmer,' 'Knecht Ruprecht') and the Manfred Overture (first piece at Bernstein's 1943 Carnegie Hall debut).

cards.front: Composer: married Clara Wieck in 1840 (his 'Year of Song'); expressed himself through fictional alter egos Florestan (fiery) and Eusebius (lyrical); co-founded the Neue Zeitschrift für Musik

Composer: championed Chopin with Eusebius exclaiming 'Hats off, gentlemen, a genius!' in a review of Chopin's variations on 'Là ci darem la mano'; published 'New Paths' endorsing the young Brahms in 1853

Composer: attempted suicide by jumping into the Rhine; died at the Endenich asylum (1856); his last piece, Ghost Variations, was based on a melody he thought was dictated by ghosts but was from his own D minor Violin Concerto

Étude: set of 21 piano miniatures on an ASCH cryptogram; depicts commedia dell'arte characters alongside 'Chiarina' (Clara), 'Estrella,' Paganini, and Chopin; contains a never-to-be-played 'Sphinxes' section; ends with a 'March of the Davidsbündler'
Song Cycle: 16 Heine songs; opens with 'Im wunderschönen Monat Mai' (ends on an unresolved dominant 7th); 'Ich grolle nicht' (C major chords); last song imagines casting a coffin into the sea
Étude: 13 short piano pieces depicting a child's world; most famous movement is 'Träumerei' ('Dreaming')
Étude: piano fantasy in C major; first movement has an 'Im Legenden-ton' middle section and ends with a coda quoting Beethoven's An die ferne Geliebte; originally titled 'Ruins'

Concerto: in A minor; premiered by Clara Wieck; begins with a tutti chord, three bars of solo piano, two more tutti chords, and a woodwind chorale from the oboe; main theme contains a cipher for 'Chiara'

Concerto: E-flat major chamber work; first movement opens with a minor-seventh leap (E-flat to D-flat); second movement is a funeral march; finale has a double fugue; influenced Brahms's Piano Quintet
Symphony: 'Rhenish' symphony in E-flat; five movements; inspired by Cologne Cathedral; fourth movement features the trombones' first entrance — a solemn chorale in E-flat minor
Symphony: C major symphony — the only homotonal 19th-century symphony (all movements in C); Mendelssohn conducted the premiere; second-movement scherzo unusually has two trios
Composer: two different song cycles both titled Liederkreis — Op. 24 sets Heine, Op. 39 sets Eichendorff; both composed during Year of Song 1840
Composer: Toccata in C originally called 'Fantastic Study in Double Notes' — Schumann claimed it was 'the hardest piece ever written'

recursive_suggestions: Carnaval (Auditory Fine Arts) — rich enough for its own deep-dive as an independent answerline
Dichterliebe (Auditory Fine Arts) — same; frequently clued independently
Clara Schumann (Auditory Fine Arts) — Robert's wife and pianist; appears in many Schumann clues and has her own significant compositional output
Rhenish Symphony (Auditory Fine Arts) — may appear as an independent answerline at higher difficulties
